channel.title: Statistics on Zhanxiong's Blog
channel.description: Recent content in Statistics on Zhanxiong's Blog

item.title: On Definitions and the Interpretation of Partial Correlation
item.description:
$\newcommand{\real}{\mathbb{R}}$ $\newcommand{\bX}{\boldsymbol{X}}$ $\newcommand{\Cov}{\operatorname{Cov}}$ $\newcommand{\diag}{\operatorname{diag}}$ $\newcommand{\sp}{\operatorname{\overline{sp}}}$ $\newcommand{\tr}{\operatorname{tr}}$

Zhanxiong’s Definition

First let me present the (seemingly) most general mathematical definition of the partial correlation of a random vector $\bX_1 \in \real^p$ given another random vector $\bX_2 \in \real^q$. This definition I presented here incorporated three sources: Brockwell and Davis ([1]), Mulaik ([2]), and Muirhead ([3]). Later I will explain how my definition connects to definitions proposed by these authors.

Definition (Partial Correlation). Let $\bX_1 \in \real^p$ and $\bX_2 \in \real^q$ be two non-degenerate zero-mean random vectors with covariance matrix $\begin{bmatrix} \Sigma_{11} & \Sigma_{12} \\ \Sigma_{21} & \Sigma_{22} \end{bmatrix}$, where $\Sigma_{11}$ and $\Sigma_{22}$ are order $p$ and order $q$ matrices respectively. Denote by $\Sigma_{11 \cdot 2} \in \real^{p \times p}$ the variance-covariance matrix of $\bX_1 - P_{\sp(\bX_2)}(\bX_1)$, where $P_{\sp(\bX_2)}(\bX_1)$ is the best linear predictor of $\bX_1$ in terms of $\bX_2 := (X_{21}, \ldots, X_{2q})$ (see [1], Definition 2.7.4). The partial correlation (matrix) of $\bX_1$ given (or adjusted for, or after partialling out) $\bX_2$ is defined as $$ \begin{equation*} \rho_{11 \cdot 2} = \diag(\Sigma_{11\cdot 2})^{-1/2}\Sigma_{11\cdot 2}\diag(\Sigma_{11\cdot 2})^{-1/2}. \end{equation*} $$

A Closed-form Expression of $\rho_{11\cdot 2}$ (in $L^2$ space)

In Definition 2.7.4 of [1], $\bX_1$ is a scalar-valued random variable, i.e., $p = 1$, under which case $P_{\sp(\bX_2)}(X_1)$ is naturally defined as the projection ([1], Theorem 2.3.1) of $X_1$ onto the closed subspace $\sp(\bX_2)$ in $L^2(\Omega, \mathscr{F}, P)$. When $\bX_1$ is a random vector from $\Omega$ to $\real^p$, the classical inner product space $L^2(\Omega, \mathscr{F}, P)$ needs to be generalized to $L^2(\Omega, \mathscr{F}, P; \real^p)$, which consists of all $X$ such that $E[\|X\|^2] = \int_\Omega \|X\|^2 dP < \infty$. Analogously, the inner product in $L^2(\Omega, \mathscr{F}, P; \real^p)$ can be defined as $\langle \xi, \eta\rangle = E[\xi^\top\eta]$. Since a linear predictor of $\bX_1$ in terms of $\bX_2$ by definition is $B\bX_2$ for some non-random matrix $B \in \real^{p \times q}$ (or a linear transformation $B \in L(\real^q, \real^p)$), the best linear predictor of $\bX_1$ in terms of $\bX_2$, say $\hat{\bX}_1 = B_0\bX_2$, must minimize the squared-norm $\|\bX_1 - B\bX_2\|^2$ in $L^2(\Omega, \mathscr{F}, P; \real^p)$ over $B \in \real^{p \times q}$. Furthermore, since

$$ \begin{align*} \|\bX_1 - B\bX_2\|^2 &= E[(\bX_1 - B\bX_2)^\top(\bX_1 - B\bX_2)] = E[\tr\left((\bX_1 - B\bX_2)(\bX_1 - B\bX_2)^\top\right)] \\ &= \tr\left(\Sigma_{11} - B\Sigma_{21} - \Sigma_{12}B^\top + B\Sigma_{22}B^\top\right) = \tr\left(\Sigma_{11} - 2B\Sigma_{21} + B\Sigma_{22}B^\top\right) \\ &= \tr\left(\Sigma_{11} - 2A\Sigma_{22}^{-1/2}\Sigma_{21} + AA^\top\right) \tag{Set $A = B\Sigma_{22}^{1/2}$} \\ &= \tr\left((A - \Sigma_{12}\Sigma_{22}^{-1/2})(A - \Sigma_{12}\Sigma_{22}^{-1/2})^\top + \Sigma_{11} - \Sigma_{12}\Sigma_{22}^{-1}\Sigma_{21} \right) \\ &\geq \tr\left(\Sigma_{11} - \Sigma_{12}\Sigma_{22}^{-1}\Sigma_{21} \right) \end{align*}
$$ with the equality holds if and only if $A = \Sigma_{12}\Sigma_{22}^{-1/2}$, or equivalently $B = \Sigma_{12}\Sigma_{22}^{-1}$, we conclude that $B_0 = \Sigma_{12}\Sigma_{22}^{-1}$. Consequently, it follows that $$ \rho_{11\cdot 2} = \diag(\Sigma_{11} - \Sigma_{12}\Sigma_{22}^{-1}\Sigma_{21})^{-1/2}(\Sigma_{11} - \Sigma_{12}\Sigma_{22}^{-1}\Sigma_{21})\diag(\Sigma_{11} - \Sigma_{12}\Sigma_{22}^{-1}\Sigma_{21})^{-1/2}. \tag{1} $$

Special Cases and Discussion

Perhaps the most important special case of the general formula (1) is the “partial correlation coefficient between two variables $\xi$ and $\eta$ given another $k$ variables $Z_1, \ldots, Z_k$”, which is of primary interest in (linear) regression models where $\xi \leftarrow Y$, $\eta \leftarrow X_0$ for one explanatory variable, and $(Z_1, \ldots, Z_k)$ is assigned to be remaining explanatory variables entering the model. Denote the variance-covariance matrix of $(\xi, \eta, Z_1, \ldots, Z_k)$ by $$ \Sigma = \begin{bmatrix} \sigma_{\xi\xi} & \sigma_{\xi\eta} & \sigma_{\xi 1} & \cdots & \sigma_{\xi k} \\ \sigma_{\eta\xi} & \sigma_{\eta\eta} & \sigma_{\eta 1} & \cdots & \sigma_{\eta k} \\ \sigma_{\xi 1} & \sigma_{\eta 1} & \sigma_{11} & \cdots & \sigma_{1k} \\ \vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ \sigma_{\xi k} & \sigma_{\eta k} & \sigma_{1k} & \cdots & \sigma_{kk}
\end{bmatrix} =: \begin{bmatrix} \Sigma_{11} & \Sigma_{12} \\ \Sigma_{21} & \Sigma_{22} \end{bmatrix}, $$ where $\Sigma_{11}$ is the northwestern order $2$ block matrix in $\Sigma$. The partial correlation between $\xi$ and $\eta$ given $(Z_1, \ldots, Z_k)$ is then intuitively defined as the $(1, 2)$ (or $(2, 1)$) entry of the matrix in (1), namely, with $e_1 = (1, 0)^\top$ and $e_2 = (0, 1)^\top$: $$ \begin{align*} \rho_{\xi\eta \cdot Z_1\cdots Z_k} &:= \frac{e_1^\top(\Sigma_{11} - \Sigma_{12}\Sigma_{22}^{-1}\Sigma_{21})e_2} {\sqrt{e_1^\top(\Sigma_{11} - \Sigma_{12}\Sigma_{22}^{-1}\Sigma_{21})e_1 \cdot e_2^\top(\Sigma_{11} - \Sigma_{12}\Sigma_{22}^{-1}\Sigma_{21})e_2}} \\ &= \frac{M_{12}/\det(\Sigma_{22})}{\sqrt{M_{22}/\det(\Sigma_{22}) \cdot M_{11}/\det(\Sigma_{22})}} = \frac{M_{12}}{\sqrt{M_{11}M_{22}}}, \tag{2} \end{align*} $$ where $M_{ij}$ denotes the $(i, j)$ minor of $\Sigma$. (2) agrees with Equation (4.46) in [2]. The penultimate step in the derivation above follows from the Schur’s identity $\det(A) = \det(A_{22})\det(A_{11} - A_{12}A_{22}^{-1}A_{21})$ for a block matrix $\begin{bmatrix} A_{11} & A_{12} \\ A_{21} & A_{22}\end{bmatrix}$ in which $A_{22}$ is invertible.

How to Interpret the Qualifier “Partial”?

References

[1] Brockwell, Peter J., Davis, Richard A. (2006). Time Series: Theory and Methods (2nd ed.). Springer Science+Business Media, LLC.

[2] Mulaik, Stanley A. (2009). Foundations of Factor Analysis. CRC Press.

[3] Muirhead, Robb J. (1982). Aspects of Multivariate Statistical Theory. John Wiley & Sons, Inc.

item.title: A Clarification on the Linear Algebra of Principal Component Analysis
item.description:
$$\newcommand{\argmax}{\operatorname*{argmax}} $$

Background

Principal Component Analysis (PCA) is a dimension reduction technique that receives applications in many scientific disciplines.
Nevertheless, I have seen so many non-rigorous, if not completely wrong, usages on its associated terminologies, math derivations, interpretations, etc. The aim of this article is to clarify some most fundamental concepts and definitions related to PCA.

Principal Component Directions vs. Principal Components

This is arguably the most serious misconception in PCA, even experienced statisticians and machine learning engineers confused them: practitioners tend to referring “principal component directions” as “principal components” or vice versa (e.g. [1], p. 224). Such confusion is very analogous to linear algebra students who cannot distinguish “basis” and “coordinates (under that basis)” clearly. A deep understanding on this linear algebra topic would help clarify on the differences and connections between “PC directions” and “PCs”.

To understand the core idea underlying the PCA, it is also important to clarify the meaning of the term “variance” under the PCA context. The “variance” appeared in the sentence such as “we are looking for the direction $v_1$ such that the variance of $Xv_1$ is maximized” should be interpreted as the sample variance of the dataset $D_1 := \{\langle x_1, v_1\rangle, \ldots, \langle x_n, v_1\rangle\}$. In other words, the PCA does not require assuming any underlying distribution of the data matrix $X = \begin{bmatrix} x_1^\top \\ \vdots \\ x_n^\top \end{bmatrix}$, as most statistical models do. This means that the PCA can be fully explained and demonstrated by linear algebra tools only without invoking any probability or statistics knowledge. In fact, as will be seen later, the term “variance” is completely superfluous and may be replaced with the more accurate and expressive term “norm”.

That said, let’s lay out classical definitions and procedures related to the PCA:

Let $X = \begin{bmatrix} x_{ij}\end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} x_1^\top \\ \vdots \\ x_n^\top \end{bmatrix} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times p}$ be a centered design matrix such that $\sum\limits_{i = 1}^n x_{ij} = 0$ for every $j \in \{1, \ldots, p\}$ (namely, $e^\top X = 0^\top$ where $e := (1, \ldots, 1) \in \mathbb{R}^n$). By definition, the first principal component direction $v_1$ is a unit vector in $\mathbb{R}^p$ such that the Euclidean norm of the vector $(x_1^\top v, \ldots, x_n^\top v)^\top =: Xv$ is maximized over the unit sphere $\{v: \|v\| = 1\}$. In other words,

$$ v_1 = \argmax_{\|v\| = 1} \|Xv\|^2 = \argmax_{\|v\| = 1} v^\top X^\top X v.$$ The vector $z_1 = Xv_1 = (x_1^\top v_1, \ldots, x_n^\top v_1)^\top \in \mathbb{R}^n$, which may be regarded as the collection of magnitudes of projections $x_1, \ldots, x_n$ onto the direction $v_1$, is called the first principal component of $X$.

Having found $v_1, \ldots, v_{j - 1} \in \mathbb{R}^p$, the $j$-th principal component direction $v_j$ is defined as (where $L(v_1, \ldots, v_{j - 1})$ is the subspace spanned by $\{v_1, \ldots, v_{j - 1}\}$): $$ v_j = \argmax_{\substack{\|v\| = 1 \\ v \perp L(v_1, \ldots, v_{j - 1})}} \|Xv\|^2, \quad j = 2, \ldots, p.$$ Accordingly, $z_j = Xv_j$ is called the $j$-th principal component of $X$.

From the pure linear algebra perspective, the “centered” condition of $X$ is not essential – without imposing it, $v_1, \ldots, v_n$ can be defined in exactly the same manner. The reason of requiring it is purely statistical: it is this condition that permits us to interpret the quadratic form $v^\top X^\top X v$ as “variance of the projected data” because the sample mean of $\{x_1^\top v, \ldots, x_n^\top v\}$ is given by $\bar{z} := \sum_{i = 1}^n x_i^\top v/n = e^\top Xv/n = 0$ whence $$ \begin{align*} v^\top X^\top Xv = \sum_{i = 1}^n(x_i^\top v - \bar{z})^2 = (n - 1)\hat{\sigma}^2(x_1^\top v, \ldots, x_n^\top v). \end{align*} $$

That $v_1, \ldots, v_p$ defined above are $p$ orthonormal eigenvectors of the matrix $X^\top X$ is a classical linear algebra result so I won’t repeat the proof here (for example, see [2], Section 7.6, Theorem 4). It is also well-known that $v_j^\top X^\top Xv_j$ equals to the $j$-th eigenvalue of $X^\top X$, where $\lambda_1 \geq \lambda_2 \geq \cdots \geq \lambda_p > 0$.

As mentioned earlier and should be crystal clear now, the principal component directions $\{v_1, \ldots, v_p\}$ form an orthonormal basis of $\mathbb{R}^p$, whereas the principal components are collections of magnitudes of projected observations onto all the basis vectors. Although this is not strictly the same as the relationship between “basis” and “coordinates” in linear algebra, the principal components do play the role of coordinates when we try to express an original datum under the new orthonormal basis (i.e., the principal component directions). For instance, we can talk about the coordinate of $x_i$ under $V := \{v_1, \ldots, v_p\}$, which can be expressed in terms of principal components as follows: $$ \begin{align*} x_i = \langle x_i, v_1\rangle v_1 + \cdots + \langle x_i, v_p\rangle v_p = z_1[i]v_1 + \cdots + z_p[i]v_p, \tag{1} \end{align*} $$ where $z[i]$ denotes the $i$-th component of $z$.

By comparison, under the natural basis $\{\varepsilon_1, \ldots, \varepsilon_p\}$ of $\mathbb{R}^p$, one has: $$ \begin{align*} x_i = \langle x_i, \varepsilon_1\rangle \varepsilon_1 + \cdots + \langle x_i, \varepsilon_p\rangle \varepsilon_p = x_i[1]\varepsilon_1 + \cdots + x_i[p]\varepsilon_p. \tag{2} \end{align*} $$ Comparing (1) and (2) would make a better sense of interpreting principal components as “coordinates”: in (2), $(x_i[1], \ldots, x_i[p])$ is exactly the coordinate of the vector $x_i$ under $\{\varepsilon_1, \ldots, \varepsilon_p\}$. Albeit the coordinate $(z_1[i], \ldots, z_p[i])$ of $x_i$ under $V$ is not any single principal component $z_j$ (which by definition is $n$-dimensional), it can be and should be interpreted as $(\pi_i(z_1), \ldots, \pi_i(z_p))$, where $\pi_i: \mathbb{R}^n \to \mathbb{R}$ is the $i$-th coordinate function (see [3], p. 88). That is, the coordinate of $x_i$ under $V$ is a function of combined principal components $\{z_1, \ldots, z_p\}$. In this sense, the principal components may still be loosely understood as coordinates with respect to the new basis $V$.

Determining PC Directions via SVD

In the machine learning literature, PC directions are usually determined by finding the singular value decomposition (SVD) of $X$ directly (as opposed to computing $X^\top X$ first, then finding the spectral decomposition of $X^\top X$, which is the way I learned as a undergraduate student). More explicitly, we have

Let $X = U\Sigma V^\top$ be the SVD of $X$, where $U \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ and $V \in \mathbb{R}^{p \times p}$ are orthogonal matrices. Then the $p$ columns of $V$ are all the $p$ PC directions with respect to $X$.

The proof is straightforward: Given $X = U\Sigma V^\top$, it follows that $X^\top X = V \Sigma^\top U^\top U\Sigma V^\top = V\Sigma^\top\Sigma V^\top$, namely, the spectral decomposition of $X^\top X$ is $V\Sigma^\top\Sigma V^\top$. This means that columns of $V$ are all the eigenvectors of $X^\top X$, thereby they are $p$ principal component directions with respect to $X$.

Further Discussions

PCA is usually taught directly in terms of the definition above, but it also has a classical routine that gives its population-level formulation first. For a given $p$-dimensional zero-mean random vector $X = (X_1, \ldots, X_p)^\top$ with covariance matrix $\Sigma$, the first population-level principle component direction $\upsilon_1$ is a unit vector in $\mathbb{R}^p$ such that the variance of the standardized linear combination of $X$ is maximized, namely, \begin{equation*} \upsilon_1 = \argmax_{\|\upsilon\| = 1}\operatorname{Var}(\upsilon^\top X) = \argmax_{\|\upsilon\| = 1}\upsilon^\top\Sigma\upsilon. \end{equation*} It seems not very intuitive why maximizing $\operatorname{Var}(\upsilon^\top X)$ is chosen to be the objective function (at least to me). In comparison, it is much more intuitive to choose the unit direction $\upsilon$ such that the expected distance (aka, norm) in $\mathbb{R}^p$ between $X$ and its projection onto the space $L(\upsilon)$, which equals to $\langle X, \upsilon\rangle\upsilon = \upsilon^\top X \upsilon = \upsilon\upsilon^\top X$, is minimized. That is, $\upsilon_1$ minimizes \begin{equation*} E[\|X - \upsilon\upsilon^\top X\|^2] = E[\|(I_{(p)} - \upsilon\upsilon^\top)X\|^2] = E[X^\top(I_{(p)} - \upsilon\upsilon^\top)X] = \operatorname{tr}((I_{(p)} - \upsilon\upsilon^\top)\Sigma) = p - \upsilon^\top\Sigma\upsilon. \end{equation*} Therefore to minimize $\|X - \upsilon\upsilon^\top X\|^2$ is equivalent to maximize $\upsilon^\top\Sigma\upsilon$. At the population-level, it seems hard to find the counterpart of the “first principal component $z_1$”, probably due to that $z_1$ is observation-dependent (for instance, it is $n$-dimensional, where $n$ is sample size). If we have to, it might not be a bad idea to define $Z_1 := \upsilon_1^\top X$ as the first population-level principal component, but it is a scalar.

Now suppose we want to approximate $X$ by a projection of $X$ onto a 2-dimensional subspace $V_2$ (in $\mathbb{R}^p$) containing $L(\upsilon_1)$. Clearly, such subspace can be expressed as $L(\upsilon_1, \upsilon)$ with $\upsilon \perp \upsilon_1$ and $\|\upsilon_1\| = 1$, and the projection of $X$ (at this moment simply view $X$ as a vector in $\mathbb{R}^p$ instead of a random vector in the $L^2$ space) onto $L(\upsilon_1, \upsilon)$ is $\hat{X} = \langle\upsilon_1, X\rangle\upsilon_1 + \langle\upsilon, X\rangle\upsilon = (\upsilon_1\upsilon_1^\top + \upsilon\upsilon^\top)X$. As before, a reasonable choice of $\upsilon_2$ should be such that the expected distance between $X$ and $\hat{X}$ is minimized, i.e., $\upsilon_2$ minimizes \begin{equation*} E[\|X - \hat{X}\|^2] = E[X^\top(I_{(p)} - \upsilon_1\upsilon_1^\top - \upsilon\upsilon^\top)X] = p - \upsilon_1^\top\Sigma\upsilon_1 - \upsilon^\top\Sigma\upsilon. \end{equation*} Hence $\upsilon_2 = \argmax_{\|\upsilon\| = 1, \upsilon \perp \upsilon_1}\upsilon^\top\Sigma\upsilon$.

The population-level principal component directions $\upsilon_3, \ldots, \upsilon_p$ can be obtained as their standard forms by a similar argument.

Now that we have defined $p$ population-level PC directions by maximizing the quadratic form $\upsilon^\top\Sigma\upsilon$ under different constraints, it is convenient to derive sample-level PC directions (those we are familiar with) by replacing “$\Sigma$” in the objective function with its estimate $\mathcal{X}^\top\mathcal{X}/n$, where $\mathcal{X} = [x_{ij}] \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times p}$ is a centered data matrix (confusingly, the $\mathcal{X}$ here is exactly the “$X$” in preceding sections). This classical recipe ubiquitously adopted in statistical inference then brings us back to the definition of “principal component directions” in the definition in Section 2.

What is less transparent is the connection between the “population-level PC $Z_j$” (later we will update its definition) and the “sample-level PC $z_j$”. To this end, let \begin{equation*} Z := \begin{bmatrix} Z_1 \\ Z_2 \\ \vdots \\ Z_p \end{bmatrix} = \Upsilon^\top X \tag{$\dagger$} \end{equation*} be the PC transformation of $X$ ([4], Theorem 11.1), which is well-defined at the population-level. From another (more easily generalizable) perspective, we view $\Upsilon^\top$ as a linear transformation from $\mathbb{R}^p$ to $\mathbb{R}^p$, which sends the coordinate of $X$ under the standard basis $\mathscr{B}_1 = \{\varepsilon_1, \ldots, \varepsilon_p\}$ to the coordinate of $X$ under the new orthonormal basis $\mathscr{B}_2 = \{\upsilon_1, \ldots, \upsilon_p\}$. In linear algebra terminology, since $\Upsilon$ is the transition matrix from $\mathscr{B}_1$ to $\mathscr{B}_2$, $(\dagger)$ is exactly the coordinate transformation equation ([2], Theorem 4.4.2) from $\mathscr{B}_1$ to $\mathscr{B}_2$ (note $\Upsilon$ is orthogonal so $\Upsilon^{-1} = \Upsilon^\top$).

But we know principal components are vectors in $\mathbb{R}^n$, where $n$ is the number of observations drawn from $X$. As such, for $n$ i.i.d. random vectors $X^{(1)}, \ldots, X^{(n)} \sim X$, let $Z^{(i)} = \begin{bmatrix} Z_1^{(i)} & \cdots & Z_p^{(i)}\end{bmatrix}^\top$ be the coordinate of $X^{(i)}$ with respect to $\mathscr{B}_2$, $i = 1, \ldots, n$. Then we have \begin{align*} \begin{bmatrix} X^{(1)} & \cdots & X^{(n)}\end{bmatrix} &= \begin{bmatrix} \varepsilon_1 & \cdots & \varepsilon_p \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} X_1^{(1)} & \cdots & X_1^{(n)} \\ X_2^{(1)} & \cdots & X_2^{(n)} \\ \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ X_p^{(1)} & \cdots & X_p^{(n)}\end{bmatrix} =: \begin{bmatrix} \varepsilon_1 & \cdots & \varepsilon_p \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \boldsymbol{X}_1^\top \\ \vdots \\ \boldsymbol{X}_p^\top \end{bmatrix} \\ &= \begin{bmatrix} \upsilon_1 & \cdots & \upsilon_p\end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} Z_1^{(1)} & \cdots & Z_1^{(n)} \\ Z_2^{(1)} & \cdots & Z_2^{(n)} \\ \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ Z_p^{(1)} & \cdots & Z_p^{(n)}\end{bmatrix} =: \begin{bmatrix} \upsilon_1 & \cdots & \upsilon_p \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \boldsymbol{Z}_1^\top \\ \vdots \\ \boldsymbol{Z}_p^\top \end{bmatrix}. \end{align*} This implies that $\begin{bmatrix} \boldsymbol{Z}_1 & \cdots & \boldsymbol{Z}_p \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \boldsymbol{X}_1 & \cdots & \boldsymbol{X}_p\end{bmatrix}\Upsilon$. So if we view $\{\boldsymbol{X}_1, \ldots, \boldsymbol{X}_p\}$ as a basis of the $p$-dimensional subspace $S$ in $\mathbb{R}^n$ (as for the geometry of linear models), this means that $\{\boldsymbol{Z}_1, \ldots, \boldsymbol{Z}_p\}$ is also a basis of $S$. Therefore, at the (semi-)population-level, $\{\boldsymbol{Z}_1, \ldots, \boldsymbol{Z}_p\}$ may be recognized as a new basis of $S$ which is obtained by rotating the original basis $\{\boldsymbol{X}_1, \ldots, \boldsymbol{X}_p\}$. The sample-level principal components $\{z_1, \ldots, z_p\}$ thus can be regarded as its sample-level counterpart after estimating

Lastly, let’s just mention an interesting representation of $z_j$ which is $z_j = \sqrt{\lambda_j}u_j$, where $u_j$ is the $j$-th column of $U$ in the SVD of $\mathcal{X} = U\Lambda V^\top = \sum_{l = 1}^p \sqrt{\lambda_l}u_lv_l^\top$ (assume $\operatorname{rank}(\mathcal{X}) = p$). The proof is easy: by definition, \begin{equation} z_j = \mathcal{X}v_j = \sum_{l = 1}^p\sqrt{\lambda_l}u_lv_l^\top v_j = \sqrt{\lambda_j}u_j. \tag{3} \end{equation}

Comparing (3) with the $j$-th principal component direction $v_j$, it appears that the PC direction and the PC itself possess some sort of duality.

Conclusion

Unlike the traditional perspective of considering PCA as the most important dimension-reduction technique, I am more inclined to view it as a data-driven basis-searching algorithm that enjoys the aforementioned properties. It is precisely the orthogonality and the rank ordering of the new basis that endows the merits of the PCA in applications.

References

[1] Géron, A. (2022). Hands-on Machine Learning with Scikit-Learn, Keras, and TensorFlow (3rd ed.). O’Reilly Media.

[2] Li, J. and Zha, J. (1988). Linear Algebra (in Chinese). University of Science and Technology of China Press.

[3] Rudin, W. (1976). Principles of Mathematical Analysis (3rd ed.). McGraw-Hill, Inc.

[4] Härdle, W.K., Simar, L. and Fengler M.R. (2024). Applied Multivariate Statistical Analysis (6th ed.). Springer International Publishing.

item.title: An Alternative Representation of $R^2$
item.description:
Problem Statement

This question was originally posted on CrossValidated. In more compact notations, it asks:

Given a linear model $$ \begin{align*} Y := \begin{bmatrix} y_1 \\ y_2 \\ \vdots \\ y_n \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} e & x_1 & x_2 & \cdots & x_p \end{bmatrix}\beta + \varepsilon, \end{align*} $$ show that the well-known coefficient of determination has the following representation: $$ \begin{align*} R^2 = r_{YX}^\top r_{XX}^{-1}r_{YX}, \end{align*} $$ where $$ \begin{align*} r_{YX} := \begin{bmatrix} r_{Y, x_1} \\ \vdots \\ r_{Y, x_p} \end{bmatrix}, \quad r_{XX} := \begin{bmatrix} r_{x_i, x_j} \end{bmatrix}. \end{align*} $$

In other words, $R^2$ is a function of pair-wise Pearson correlations, which is surprisingly elegant.

The Proof

To begin with, rewrite the linear model as $$ \begin{align*} Y := \begin{bmatrix} y_1 \\ y_2 \\ \vdots \\ y_n \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} e & x_1 & x_2 & \cdots & x_p \end{bmatrix}\beta + \varepsilon := \begin{bmatrix} e & \tilde{X}\end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} \beta_0 \\ \gamma \end{bmatrix} + \varepsilon, \tag{1} \end{align*} $$ where $x_j = \begin{bmatrix} x_{1j} & \cdots & x_{nj}\end{bmatrix}^\top$, $j = 1, \ldots, p$, $\beta_0 \in \mathbb{R}$ is the coefficient corresponding to $e$, $\gamma \in \mathbb{R}^p$ is the coefficient corresponding to $\tilde{X} = \begin{bmatrix} x_1 & \cdots & x_p\end{bmatrix} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times p}$.

The proposed form of $R^2$ inspires me considering the following representation of a regression model (known as Standardized Multiple Regression Model, see Applied Linear Statistical Models by Kutner et al., Section 7.5) – this is how I managed to bring all the Pearson correlations to the party: $$ \begin{align*} Y^* = X^*\beta^* + \varepsilon^*, \tag{2} \end{align*} $$

where $$ \begin{align*} Y^* = \begin{bmatrix} \frac{y_1 - \bar{y}}{\sqrt{n - 1}s_Y} \\ \vdots \\ \frac{y_n - \bar{y}}{\sqrt{n - 1}s_Y} \end{bmatrix} = (\sqrt{n - 1}s_Y)^{-1}(I - n^{-1}ee^\top)Y \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times 1}, \tag{3.1} \end{align*} $$

$$ \begin{align*} X^* = \begin{bmatrix} \frac{x_1 - \bar{x}_1e}{\sqrt{n - 1}s_1} & \cdots & \frac{x_p - \bar{x}_pe}{\sqrt{n - 1}s_p} \end{bmatrix} = (I - n^{-1}ee^\top)\tilde{X}(\sqrt{n - 1}\Lambda)^{-1} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times p}, \tag{3.2} \end{align*} $$

$$ \begin{align*} \bar{y} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i = 1}^ny_i, \quad s_Y^2 = \frac{1}{n - 1}\sum_{i = 1}^n(y_i - \bar{y})^2, \tag{3.3} \end{align*} $$

$$ \begin{align*} \bar{x}_j = n^{-1}\sum_{i = 1}^n x_{ij}, \quad s_j^2 = \frac{1}{n - 1}\sum_{i = 1}^n(x_{ij} - \bar{x}_j)^2, j = 1, \ldots, p, \tag{3.4} \end{align*} $$

$$ \begin{align*} \Lambda = \operatorname{diag}(s_1, \ldots, s_p) \in \mathbb{R}^{p \times p}. \tag{3.5} \end{align*} $$

In words, $Y^*$ and $X^*$ are simply standardized versions of original inputs $Y$ and $\tilde{X}$.

With the above notations, on one hand, it is easy to verify that $$ \begin{align*} r_{YX} := \begin{bmatrix} r_{Y, x_1} \\ \vdots \\ r_{Y, x_p} \end{bmatrix} = X^{*\top}Y^*, \quad r_{XX} := \begin{bmatrix} r_{x_i, x_j} \end{bmatrix} = X^{*\top}X^*, \tag{4} \end{align*} $$ hence $$ \begin{align*} \hat{\beta^*} = (X^{*\top}X^*)^{-1}X^{*\top}Y^* = r_{XX}^{-1}r_{YX}. \end{align*} $$

On the other hand, one can express the OLS estimate of $\beta$ in $(1)$ in terms of the OLS estimate of $\beta^*$ in $(2)$ as follows (this is easy to verify by substituting transformation definitions $(3.1)$ – $(3.5)$ into $(2)$ then compare it with $(1)$, see also the aforementioned reference for derivation details): $$ \begin{align*} & \hat{\gamma} = s_Y\Lambda^{-1}\hat{\beta^*} = s_Y\Lambda^{-1}r_{XX}^{-1}r_{YX}, \tag{5.1} \\ & \hat{\beta}_0 = \bar{Y} - \begin{bmatrix}\bar{x}_1 & \cdots & \bar{x}_p \end{bmatrix}\hat{\gamma} = n^{-1}e^\top Y - n^{-1}e^\top \tilde{X}\hat{\gamma}. \tag{5.2} \end{align*} $$

It then follows by $(5.1), (5.2), (3.1), (3.2)$ that $$ \begin{align*} Y - \hat{Y} = Y - e\hat{\beta_0} - \tilde{X}\hat{\gamma} = (I - n^{-1}ee^\top)Y - (I - n^{-1}ee^\top)\tilde{X}\hat{\gamma} = s_Y\sqrt{n - 1}Y^* - s_Y\sqrt{n - 1}X^*r_{XX}^{-1}r_{YX}, \end{align*} $$ whence by $(4)$ we conclude $$ \begin{align*} & (Y - \hat{Y})^\top(Y - \hat{Y}) \\ =& (n - 1)s_Y^2 Y^{*\top}Y^* - 2(n - 1)s_Y^2Y^{*\top}X^{*}r_{XX}^{-1}r_{YX} + (n - 1)s_Y^2r_{YX}^\top r_{XX}^{-1}X^{*\top}X^*r_{XX}^{-1}r_{YX} \\ =& (n - 1)s_Y^2(Y^{*\top}Y^* - r_{YX}^\top r_{XX}^{-1}r_{YX}). \tag{6} \end{align*} $$ It then follows by $(6)$ and the definition of $R^2$ that $$ \begin{align*} & R^2 = 1 - \frac{(Y - \hat{Y})^\top(Y - \hat{Y})}{Y^\top (I - n^{-1}ee^\top)Y} = 1 - \frac{(n - 1)s_Y^2(Y^{*\top}Y^* - r_{YX}^\top r_{XX}^{-1}r_{YX})}{(n - 1)s_Y^2 Y^{*\top}Y^*} = r_{YX}^\top r_{XX}^{-1}r_{YX}. \end{align*} $$ In the last equality, we used the identity $Y^{*\top}Y^* = 1$ (which follows from $(3.1)$ and $(3.3)$). This completes the proof.

More Details

Below is a more detailed derivation of $(5.1)$ and $(5.2)$.

By model $(1)$, we have $$ \begin{align*} \begin{bmatrix} \hat{\beta}_0 \\ \hat{\gamma} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} n & e^\top\tilde{X} \\ \tilde{X}^\top e & \tilde{X}^\top\tilde{X} \end{bmatrix}^{-1} \begin{bmatrix} e^\top Y \\ \tilde{X}^\top Y \end{bmatrix}. \tag{A.1} \end{align*} $$ Denote the matrix $\tilde{X}^\top(I - n^{-1}ee^\top)\tilde{X}$ by $C$, which by $(3.2)$ is equal to $(n - 1)\Lambda X^{*\top}X^*\Lambda$. It follows by the block matrix inversion formula that $$ \begin{align*} \begin{bmatrix} n & e^\top\tilde{X} \\ \tilde{X}^\top e & \tilde{X}^\top\tilde{X} \end{bmatrix}^{-1} = \begin{bmatrix} n^{-1} + n^{-2}e^\top\tilde{X}C^{-1}\tilde{X}^\top e & -n^{-1}e^\top\tilde{X}C^{-1} \\ -n^{-1}C^{-1}\tilde{X}^\top e & C^{-1} \end{bmatrix}. \tag{A.2} \end{align*} $$ Substituting $(A.2)$ into $(A.1)$ and using the idempotency of the matrix $I - n^{-1}ee^\top$ (also plugging definitions $(3.1)$ and $(3.2)$) then give $$ \begin{align*} & \hat{\gamma} = -n^{-1}C^{-1}\tilde{X}^\top ee^\top Y + C^{-1}\tilde{X}^\top Y \\ =& C^{-1}\tilde{X}^\top(I - n^{-1}ee^\top)Y \\ =& C^{-1}((I - n^{-1}ee^\top)\tilde{X})^\top (I - n^{-1}ee^\top)Y \\ =& (n - 1)^{-1}\Lambda^{-1}(X^{*\top}X^*)^{-1}\Lambda^{-1}(\sqrt{n - 1}X^*\Lambda)^\top \sqrt{n - 1}s_YY^* \\ =& s_Y\Lambda^{-1}(X^{*\top}X^*)^{-1}X^{*\top}Y^* \\ =& s_Y\Lambda^{-1}\hat{\beta^*}. \\[1em] & \hat{\beta}_0 = n^{-1}e^\top Y + n^{-2}e^\top \tilde{X}C^{-1}\tilde{X}^\top ee^\top Y - n^{-1}e^\top\tilde{X}C^{-1}\tilde{X}^\top Y \\ =& n^{-1}e^\top Y - n^{-1}e^\top\tilde{X}(-n^{-1}C^{-1}\tilde{X}^\top ee^\top Y + C^{-1}\tilde{X}^\top Y) \\ =& n^{-1}e^\top Y - n^{-1}e^\top\tilde{X}\hat{\gamma}, \end{align*} $$ which are exactly $(5.1)$ and $(5.2)$.

item.title: Completeness of a Normal Family
item.description:
The problem is: How to show that $\{N(\theta,1):\theta \in \Omega\}$ is not a complete family of distributions when $\Omega$ is finite?

For notational convenience, let $\varphi_{\theta}(x)$ denote the density of a $N(\theta, 1)$ random variable.

One way of viewing this problem is that the condition $$ \begin{align*} \int_{-\infty}^\infty g(x)\varphi_\theta(x)dx = 0, \quad \theta \in \{-1, 0, 1\} \tag{1} \end{align*} $$ sets up a system of three equations. If we pick up $g(x) = ax^3 + bx^2 + cx + d$ from the family of cubic polynomials, then $(1)$ becomes a homogeneous system of three linear equations with four unknowns $a, b, c, d$. By linear algebra theory, the dimensionality of the solution space to such a linear system is at least $1$ (because the rank of the associated coefficient matrix is at most $3$), implying that there are countless non-zero $g(x)$ satisfying $(1)$.

Indeed, substituting $g(x) = ax^3 + bx^2 + cx + d$ and central moments of $\varphi_\theta(x)$ into $(1)$ yields $$ \begin{align*} b + d &= 0, \\ 4a + 2b + c + d &= 0, \tag{2} \\ -4a + 2b - c + d &= 0. \end{align*} $$ One (of infinitely many) non-zero solution to $(2)$ is $a = -1, b = 0, c = 4, d = 0$, resulting $g(x) = -x^3 + 4x \neq 0$ (or more formally, $P_\theta(g(X) = 0) = P_\theta(X \in \{0, -2, 2\}) = 0 \neq 1$). This shows that the family $\{N(\theta, 1): \theta \in \Omega\}$ is not complete.

While the above construction works well for any parameter space with finite cardinality, it does not generalize to the case where the parameter space contains infinite members (e.g., the original linked exercise whose $\Omega = \{1, 2, 3, \ldots\}$). To deal with the latter case, note that, by the translation property of $\varphi_\theta$, the condition $E_\theta[g(X)] = 0$ for all $\theta \in \Omega$ is equivalent to
$$ \begin{align*} E[g(X + \theta)] = 0 \quad \text{ for all } \theta \in \Omega, \tag{3} \end{align*} $$ where “$E$” is with respect to the standard normal density $\varphi(x)$. This observation implies that, when $\Omega = \{1, 2, \ldots\}$, $(3)$ automatically holds for $g$ such that $g(x + \theta) = g(x)$ for all $\theta = 1, 2, \ldots$ and $E[g(X)] = 0$. Clearly, one candidate of such $g$ is any periodic odd function with periodicity $1$. For example, $g(x) = \sin(\pi x)$ proposed in @jld’s answer. As another (non-trigonometric) example, $g(x)$ can be taken to be $$ \begin{align*} g(x) = \frac{1}{2} - \left|x - \frac{1}{2}\right|, \quad 0 \leq x \leq 1. \end{align*} $$ And then copy this to intervals $[n, n + 1]$, $n = 1, 2, \ldots$ to complete $g$’s definition on $[0, +\infty)$. Finally, define $g(x) = -g(-x)$ when $x < 0$.
